topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers swimming before her eyes. Her fingers hovered over the mouse, trembling slightly. Liam, the new intern, had just received a bonus that dwarfed hers. A slow, tight knot formed in her chest, a sensation like a stone settling deep in her diaphragm. She forced herself to click, to examine the data, but the sheer disparity in compensation felt like a physical blow. "That's just how things work," she muttered to herself, the sound thin and brittle in the quiet office. She quickly minimized the window, turning her attention to a stack of unrelated reports, trying to bury the uncomfortable truth under layers of mundane tasks. The urge to confront Liam, to demand an explanation, warred with a sudden, overwhelming desire to simply ignore the glaring reality.

[story 2]

"Are you quite finished with that, Sarah?" Mark’s voice cut through the hum of the open-plan office. Sarah didn't look up immediately. She was meticulously re-typing figures, her jaw clenched so tightly she could feel the strain in her molars. The realization—that David, the junior analyst, was earning significantly more—had settled over her like a heavy blanket. She felt a distinct tightening around her eyes, a hardening of her resolve that made her shoulders tense beneath her blouse. When Mark pressed her, she offered a tight, practiced smile. "Just finalizing these projections, Mark. Everything is on track." Inside, a fierce, unyielding refusal to acknowledge the imbalance churned, a stubborn refusal to let this information disrupt her carefully constructed composure.

[story 3]

I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. The figures were stark. David’s salary versus mine—the gap was undeniable, a chasm between two very different realities. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I deliberately took a slow, deep breath, trying to regulate the sudden tremor in my hands. I didn't want to look at him. I didn't want to voice the question that was burning at the back of my throat. Instead, I picked up a pen and started highlighting sections of a completely different document, focusing intently on the irrelevant details. My posture became rigid, a deliberate shield against the unwelcome data. I would deal with this later, when I was ready, and for now, I would simply continue my work, moving forward regardless of the numbers.
